text:
Catalytic C–H borylation is a powerful tool for functionalizing primary and secondary sp³ C–H bonds, yet tertiary C–H bonds have remained notoriously inert under standard conditions. Here, we report a broadly applicable iridium-catalyzed method for the highly selective borylation of bridgehead tertiary C–H bonds in bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs), bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs), and heterobicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (XBCHs). Using a catalyst system comprising [Ir(COD)OMe]₂ and 2-methylphenanthroline, we achieved exclusive bridgehead functionalization across more than 35 examples, including the late-stage modification of complex pharmaceuticals and the synthesis of versatile bicyclic building blocks.

The unusual reactivity of these tertiary centers is attributed to high s-character induced by bridgehead strain, which allows them to react at rates comparable to cyclopropanes and significantly faster than unactivated alkyl C–H bonds. Kinetic and computational analyses reveal that while C–H bond cleavage is reversible and occurs with a modest barrier, the turnover-limiting step is an isomerization of the seven-coordinate Ir(V) intermediate that precedes C–B bond-forming reductive elimination. This method provides a direct, practical route to stable bridgehead boronic esters, bypassing the high-energy intermediates and multi-step sequences typically required to access these coveted three-dimensional architectural motifs.
